meta.title: Google OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Flow
meta.description: How a web application authenticates a user via Google OAuth 2.0 with PKCE, from login button click to authenticated session.
React SPA. Initiates the login flow and stores the resulting session cookie.
meta.notes: Uses window.location.href to follow the OAuth redirect chain.

meta.description: Node.js backend. Generates PKCE params, handles the callback, exchanges the code for tokens, and issues sessions.
meta.notes: Stores code_verifier in a short-lived server-side state keyed by the state param.

meta.description: Redis. Stores authenticated user sessions with a 24-hour TTL.
meta.notes: Key: session:{uuid}  Value: { userId, email, accessToken }

meta.description: accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/v2/auth — shows the consent screen and issues authorization codes.
meta.notes: Validates redirect_uri against the URIs registered for this OAuth client in Google Cloud Console.

meta.description: oauth2.googleapis.com/token — exchanges authorization codes for access tokens and ID tokens.
meta.notes: Verifies code_verifier against the code_challenge stored at authorization time.

steps.name: Overview
steps.title: Google OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code Flow
steps.description: A user signs in with Google. The browser, application server, and Google's OAuth endpoints cooperate to prove the user's identity without the app ever seeing their Google password.

steps.name: Click Sign in
steps.title: User clicks Sign in with Google
steps.description: The user triggers the login flow. The browser calls the app's login endpoint to begin the OAuth dance.

steps.name: PKCE & redirect
steps.title: App generates PKCE params and redirects
steps.description: The server generates a cryptographic code_verifier, derives a code_challenge from it, stores the verifier, and returns a 302 redirect to Google's authorization endpoint.

steps.name: Consent screen
steps.title: Browser navigates to Google consent screen
steps.description: The browser follows the redirect to Google's authorization endpoint. Google validates the client_id and redirect_uri, then renders the consent screen listing the requested scopes.

steps.name: User grants access
steps.title: User reviews and grants access
steps.description: Google's consent screen shows which data the app is requesting. The user reviews the scopes and clicks Allow. Google records the grant and prepares an authorization code.

steps.name: Auth code issued
steps.title: Google redirects back with authorization code
steps.description: Google issues a single-use authorization code that expires in 60 seconds and redirects the browser to the app's registered callback URL. The state param is echoed back for CSRF protection.

steps.name: Exchange code
steps.title: App server exchanges code for tokens
steps.description: The server verifies the state param against its stored value, retrieves the code_verifier, and POSTs to Google's token endpoint. Sending the verifier proves this is the same party that initiated the flow.

steps.name: Tokens returned
steps.title: Google returns access token and ID token
steps.description: Google verifies the code_verifier against the stored code_challenge (SHA-256 match), then issues tokens. The ID token is a signed JWT containing the user's identity claims.

steps.name: Session created
steps.title: Session created in Redis
steps.description: The server creates a new session keyed by a UUID, stores the user's identity and access token, and sets a 24-hour TTL. The session key is what will live in the browser cookie.

steps.name: Cookie set
steps.title: Session cookie set — user is authenticated
steps.description: The server writes the session ID into a secure, HttpOnly cookie and redirects the browser to the dashboard. The OAuth flow is complete. Future requests carry the cookie and are authenticated without hitting Google again.
